stem: A income study dataset contains 50 observations of monthly income. The data is believed to come from a mixture of two populations: part-time workers (mean ≈ 57.7 $, SD ≈ 9.4) and full-time workers (mean ≈ 123.7 $, SD ≈ 25.6). The proportion of part-time workers in the sample is uncertain, estimated between 32% and 43%. The observed overall mean is 98.6 $ with SD = 38.3. Estimate the true population mean of monthly income, accounting for the uncertainty in the mixing proportion.

ground_truth.answer: Mixture mean = w×57.7 + (1-w)×123.7. As w varies from 0.32 to 0.43, the mixture mean ranges from 95.3 to 102.6. With sampling variability, CI = [84.7, 113.2]. Point estimate at w=0.38: 98.6 $.

solution_steps: Mixture mean formula: μ_mix = w × μ_A + (1-w) × μ_B.
At w=0.32: μ_mix = 102.6 (more of full-time workers).
At w=0.43: μ_mix = 95.3 (more of part-time workers).
Combined interval [84.7, 113.2] accounts for both mixing uncertainty and sampling variability.
common_failure_modes: Using the simple average of the two component means without weighting
Treating the mixing proportion as known instead of uncertain
Ignoring that the overall SD (38.3) is inflated by the between-component variation
Not recognizing that the mixture variance includes both within-component and between-component variation
